sections.title: Global Equities
sections.content: Global equities fell as a renewed sell-off in mega-cap technology and AI-related shares, triggered by stretched valuations, a New York Times report that OpenAI may delay its IPO and a chip-stock rout, dragged indices lower. MSCI World approx. -1.8%, MSCI Developed Markets approx. -1.8% and MSCI Emerging Market -1.2%. The S&P 500 fell roughly 2.0% to about 7,354, with the MAG7 / mega-cap tech cohort leading the declines, while the Dow rose 0.6% and the Russell 2000 1.0% on a rotation into defensives and small caps. Europe's Euro Stoxx 50 lost about 1.2%. In Asia the Nikkei 225 fell roughly 3%, China's CSI 300 declined around 1.5% and Hong Kong's Hang Seng eased about 1.5%.

sections.title: US Equities
sections.content: US indices finished mixed as renewed weakness in large-cap technology and AI-related shares weighed on the S&P 500 and Nasdaq, the latter posting a fifth straight losing session and sliding about 4.6% on the week, its worst stretch since spring 2025, while the S&P 500 fell roughly 2% to around 7,354. A report that OpenAI may delay its IPO, alongside SpaceX's weak post-listing performance, soured AI sentiment and triggered a chip-stock rout; Micron's blockbuster earnings and 16% surge could not stem a rotation into defensives, leaving the Dow up 0.6% and the Russell 2000 up 1.0%. Macro data were firmer: the flash composite PMI climbed to a five-month high of 52.2 and manufacturing reached 55.7, the strongest since 2022, though hiring softened for a second month and input-price inflation stayed historically elevated.

sections.title: European Equities
sections.content: European equities were broadly flat as an early-week advance gave way to a Friday technology sell-off driven by concerns over stretched AI-related valuations. The pan-European STOXX Europe 600 ended essentially unchanged, up 0.04% in local-currency terms, masking wide regional dispersion. The UK's FTSE 100 outperformed, climbing 1.40% on its value and energy tilt, while Germany's DAX fell 1.26%, France's CAC 40 slipped 0.43% and Italy's FTSE MIB dropped 3.00%, leaving the Euro Stoxx 50 down around 1.2%. Lower oil prices offered some relief to the region's net importers and eased the inflation impulse, but the global repricing of technology and AI exposure dominated sentiment into the close, overshadowing an otherwise steady domestic macro backdrop and leaving defensive and energy-tilted markets relatively better placed.

sections.title: APAC Equities
sections.content: APAC markets were broadly weaker as the global technology and AI sell-off spread across the region. Japan's Nikkei 225 fell about 4.5% on Friday to end down roughly 3% on the week, hit by chip-related weakness despite Micron's strong results. South Korea's Kospi tumbled 6.9% on Friday, triggering a 20-minute circuit breaker amid heavy semiconductor selling. China's CSI 300 declined around 1.9% and Hong Kong's Hang Seng lost about 1.6%, with investors still awaiting fresh domestic stimulus. Falling oil offered a partial offset for the region's energy importers, Brent easing towards USD 74 after Saudi Aramco resumed loading at its Ras Tanura terminal following a months-long disruption. With the AI-driven tech cycle the dominant swing factor, regional risk appetite closely tracked the deteriorating mood in global mega-cap technology rather than domestic fundamentals.

sections.title: Foreign Exchange
sections.content: The US dollar extended its rally, the DXY gaining around 2% over six straight sessions since the Fed meeting as widening rate differentials reasserted themselves as the dominant FX driver. Persistent US inflation and resilient data reinforced a hawkish Fed, with markets now assigning a high probability of a hike by September, while softer eurozone growth and cooling prices reduced ECB tightening pressure. EUR/USD broke below 1.14, finding buyers near 1.1350 and leaving the euro down more than 2.5% month-to-date, its largest monthly fall in almost a year; Convera flagged a likely 1.11-1.15 range into early Q3 absent a fresh catalyst. Sterling and the yen also ceded ground to broad dollar strength, the greenback's momentum overriding the risk-off equity flows that might otherwise have lent the traditional haven currencies support.

sections.title: Commodities
sections.content: Commodities extended a broad decline, with the Bloomberg Commodity Index heading for its worst month since 2022, down around 10% in June and trimming its year-to-date gain to 15% as every sector traded lower. Energy led the slide, off roughly 16% on the month, as the US-Iran peace deal, a hawkish Fed and a stronger dollar combined to deflate the geopolitical risk premium; WTI fell towards USD 70 and Brent eased near USD 74 after Saudi Aramco resumed Ras Tanura loading. Precious metals dropped about 12%, gold pressured by the firmer dollar and an unusually strong positive correlation to the falling S&P 500, sliding into the USD 4,000-4,100 support zone. Copper held a USD 6.20-6.60 range, supported by persistent inventory drawdowns and US stockpiling ahead of a potential copper tariff announcement.

sections.title: Fixed Income
sections.content: Government bonds firmed as moderating inflation expectations relieved pressure on rates, a notable shift from the prior week's hawkish-Fed sell-off. With oil sliding towards USD 70 and energy disinflation expected to reverse a chunk of the recent price spike, the 10-year UST yield eased to around 4.38% and the 2-year to about 4.07%, steepening the curve modestly. Edward Jones noted that cheaper energy should moderate headline inflation in the coming months, tempering expectations for further Fed tightening even as policy stays restrictive. Credit tone was mixed: investment-grade corporates gained, with new issues generally oversubscribed, while high yield came under more pressure amid concerns over monetary policy, AI-driven equity valuations, heavy new supply, slowing fund inflows and a broader risk-off mood that mirrored the week's renewed equity-market caution.
